chapters.title: Feel it all
chapters.text:
Imagine that one day, you meet a genie who offers you three superpowers: flying, invisibility, and understanding what controls human behavior. Then you wake up and realize it was just a dream. Sorry, no flying in the sky or spying on your friends and colleagues. The third wish, however, is within your reach, even in this real world. The secret to understanding people is ridiculously simple — what rules over them is emotions.

Some experts might disagree. Traditionally, there's a long-standing belief that only three primary needs control human behavior — hunger, sex drive, and the will to survive. Yet, emotions can overpower these. After all, would most people willingly eat food they're disgusted by? Would they engage in sexual relationships with those who frighten them? Or would they maintain their will to live when overwhelmed by sadness and dark thoughts?

Life quality isn't about possessions but the depth of emotions experienced.

While many seek happiness by avoiding negative feelings like anger, fear, or sadness, a fulfilling life involves recognizing and experiencing the entire spectrum of emotions. And this summary will guide you in achieving just that!

chapters.title: Down but not out
chapters.text:
There's no better way to handle loss or heartbreak than allowing yourself to experience emotions like sadness and agony. So, if you feel the urge to curl up in bed and have a good cry, don't hold back — you're doing yourself a favor. Society will likely tell you to "toughen up," but ignore that advice. What many don't realize is that sadness is a necessary evolutionary tool, and here are some functions it performs:

• ​​Sadness is a message: Your face gives you away. When you're sad, the corners of your lips turn down, your eyes look downward, and your eyelids droop. These facial expressions signal to others: "I need comfort." Seeing you like this, family and friends will likely reach out and support you, which can work wonders for your emotional well-being.
• Sadness helps you understand what loss is: Some people try to suppress their agony by denying it or taking medications, both of which are very controversial. Without fully experiencing sadness, you can't grasp the nature of loss or tap into your inner strength to endure it.
• Sadness helps your body: Dealing with loss through sorrow saves your body energy. Crying is also beneficial for stress relief. Most people say they feel much better after shedding tears.

True healing isn't just about moving on — it's about thriving and growing through your experiences.

Suppose you’re a manager who has to deliver bad news to a subordinate. As you do so, you notice the subtle sadness on their face. Should you acknowledge it or let it slide? It depends on the type of person your subordinate is:

• If they’re generally not very expressive, you can say you're sorry for disappointing them. If the news concerns a failed promotion, you can offer them hope by expressing your readiness to support them next time.
• If you know that this person can become very emotional, it is better not to acknowledge even subtle facial expressions. They could get very angry or hysterical, leading to a counterproductive conversation.

chapters.title: Anger management 101
chapters.text:
Anger is arguably the most dangerous emotion, primarily due to its highly contagious nature. Imagine accidentally bumping into someone in an elevator and spilling your coffee on them; the person turns around and starts shouting at you. Your natural reaction almost goes without saying: you'll likely get angry and start engaging in a heated argument. Physically, you'll feel the urge to pull your eyebrows down and, together, open your eyes wide and press your upper teeth against your lower.

Like sadness, anger conveys a message, but it’s much harder to summarize in a single sentence. It might mean "get out of my way" as a warning to someone who provokes your anger. However, there are still shades of rage that this phrase can't capture: for instance, when your anger is a response to someone else's reaction or when you watch the news and see something outrageous.

It's okay to feel and express anger; it's another helpful emotion for identifying when someone crosses your boundaries. However, in its chaotic form, anger can be destructive and may even drive people to hurt each other verbally or physically.

Some people often merge disgust with anger, repulsed by the target they also attack. ~ Paul Ekman

If you often find yourself engulfed with rage, try the approach devised by the Dalai Lama. He recommends directing that attention not to a person but to their action. In an argument, try to understand what made the other person angry. Could it be childhood traumas resurfacing? Is anger the only emotion they know how to express? Once you shift your mindset in this direction, you might find it easier to sympathize rather than waste energy on anger.

When in doubt about how to act, choose to be compassionate toward yourself and others.

Did you know? Botox facial injections are becoming an increasingly popular way to hide aging signs. Yet paradoxically, an unanimated face is often considered less attractive to the human eye.

chapters.title: Fight, flight, or freak out
chapters.text:
Surprise has always been a controversial topic for scientists; some even refuse to identify it as an emotion. Why? Because of its fleeting nature. It is nearly impossible to catch someone on camera as they are surprised by a sudden revelation or unexpected visitors, as their emotion lasts only a few seconds. Nevertheless, surprise rarely comes alone — it often brings along other emotions, most frequently fear.

Most fears are unlearned triggers — things people might never have encountered but still dread. Physical pain is a universal fear, as is the fear of losing support, falling, or being hit by flying objects. Interestingly, the fear of snakes is also an unlearned trigger, as people are naturally programmed to fear reptiles even more than knives or guns.

Fear is at the core of many anxious moods and psychological disorders, including:
• Phobias: These are unexplained fears, such as those related to animals, injuries, death, and crowded places.
• PTSD (Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder): A reaction to a traumatic event, manifesting as reliving the trauma and avoiding related triggers.
• Panic attacks: Sudden, intense episodes of worry and anxiety, often linked to past trauma.
• Pathological anxiety: The most extreme form of anxiety, characterized by persistent and intense worry.

In your relationships, always try to acknowledge your fears. Imagine your child comes home from school, and when you ask them about their day, a slight shadow of fear and worry crosses their face. Their upper eyelids raise, and their jaw drops slightly. Was it something at school that made them anxious? Or something at home? You can't tell just by recognizing the emotion — you need to dig deeper. For example, you could ask, "Is there anything that's been bothering you lately?"

Ask open, empathetic questions to create a safe space for people to share their thoughts and feelings.

chapters.title: From childhood innocence to adult caution
chapters.text:
There's a unique group of people who show no disgust. Offer them a chocolate cake shaped like dog feces, and they will eat it with no problem at all. You might have guessed it already — these are young children. Kids develop feelings of disgust between ages four and eight when they gain the ability to recognize that an object's appearance can differ from its reality (like the chocolate cake). Until then, they're comfortable with things adults find repulsive; in fact, kids might even find these things fascinating.

As an adult, disgust serves an essential function: it steers you away from potentially harmful things, such as bad food or morally wrong actions. You can only tone down your disgust if it concerns your loved ones. You wouldn't voluntarily change a stranger's diapers, but you would for your child or a bedridden relative.

The suspension of disgust establishes intimacy and is a mark of personal commitment. ~ Paul Ekman

Facial expressions of disgust are easy to spot. The focus isn't on the eyes, which remain relaxed, but on the nose and mouth. The upper lip is usually raised, while the nose wrinkles as sensitivity in these facial areas heightens. Since disgust is a negative emotion, it's important to address it when you notice it in others.

Imagine you tell your teenage daughter she can't go out today because she needs to watch her younger sister due to an emergency. If disgust is evident on her face, empathize with her. You might say, "I see that you think I'm being unfair," or "Are you frustrated about dealing with this?" Let her express her suppressed feelings, and importantly, don't try to defend yourself in the moment. Afterward, calmly explain why this course of action is necessary without making it personal.

In conflicts, turn off your reaction center; only connecting with others will strengthen your bonds.

chapters.title: Lost in translation
chapters.text:
While most people say pursuing happiness is their primary goal, can they give it a universal definition? Most likely, no. First of all, happiness is subjective. For some, it might be a dazzling career; for others, it might be a large family or financial wealth. Second, happiness is ambiguous; a single emotion like relief or amusement can't describe it. It encompasses a whole spectrum of feelings, some of which even the English language struggles to capture.

Your happiness is unique. Find it by following what truly matters to you.

While English has familiar terms like enjoyment, relief, and ecstasy to describe emotions, other languages offer unique words for specific feelings that English lacks. For instance, consider the word fiero, an Italian word for pride. It's more than just regular pride — it's an intense emotion you feel after achieving a remarkable and challenging goal you've worked toward your entire life.

If you have kids, how do you feel when they accomplish their goals? It's pride for sure, but no word in English fully captures this parental excitement. Yet, you can find this exact word in Yiddish — naches. It's a deep, emotional resonance that combines pride, joy, fulfillment, and a profound sense of connection.

Positive emotions can also have negative connotations. For example, the German word schadenfreude refers to feeling joy or happiness due to someone else's misfortune. Western culture disapproves of this emotion, and for a good reason — while schadenfreude can improve a person’s mood, on a higher level, it contributes to moral corruption.

So far, scientists have focused on negative emotions and how they interfere with daily life. Positive feelings have often gone unnoticed, but their exploration is no less critical. For example, a strong bond exists between your personality traits and emotions.

If you're an extrovert, you tend to be more satisfied with your life and more resilient to rejection and failure. Such characteristics make you more suitable for living in American culture. If you're optimistic, there's some good news for you. Optimists often achieve more remarkable things and have excellent health, ultimately leading to longer lives. What is this, if not a sign, to bring more positive emotions into your life?

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Emotions are inevitable. No matter how much you try to hide or suppress them, they will always find a way to express themselves, sometimes even without you noticing it. But there is no reason to run away from them. Emotions are natural instruments of your body that protect you from danger, aid in quick decision-making, and help you bond with others. For these reasons, try acknowledging and embracing them as a necessary part of your life.

Try this

• Start journaling: Writing about your feelings provides insights into your emotional well-being.
• Express yourself: Channel your emotions into artistic activities like writing, drawing, or dancing.
• Practice mindfulness: Try breathing techniques or meditation to regulate your body.

Read it till the end?
